The quality of agent output is bounded by the quality of the instructions that produced it.
techniques.implementation: Structure instructions with explicit sections: objective (what to produce), constraints (what to avoid), format (how to structure output), scope (boundaries of the task), and success criteria (how to evaluate completion). Test instructions by verifying that a fresh agent with no prior context can execute them correctly. Iterate based on output quality. When output is wrong, examine the instruction before blaming the agent.
success_indicators: Fresh agents produce correct output from instructions alone without clarification
Instructions are reusable across sessions and model versions
Output quality is consistent across repeated executions of the same instruction
Instruction failures are diagnosed and improved systematically
failure_modes: Implicit context: instructions assume knowledge the agent does not have, producing output that looks correct but is wrong
Over-specification: instructions so detailed they prevent the agent from applying judgment where judgment would improve output
Under-specification: instructions so vague that output quality depends on interpretation rather than instruction clarity
Static instructions: never revised despite consistent output quality issues, treating bad results as agent failures rather than instruction failures

techniques.name: Error Recovery Protocol
techniques.description: Defined procedures for detecting, containing, and recovering from agent errors during execution. Agents will produce incorrect output, corrupt state, exceed scope, and misinterpret instructions, and they will do so regularly. Error recovery protocols ensure that when failures occur, the response is systematic rather than ad hoc: detect the error, stop propagation, assess blast radius, restore to known good state, and document the incident for prevention.
techniques.implementation: Define error categories (output quality failure, state corruption, scope violation, resource exhaustion) with specific detection criteria and recovery procedures for each. Implement checkpoints at natural boundaries in complex tasks. Establish rollback procedures for every operation that modifies shared state. Document recovery steps so they can be executed by any team member.
success_indicators: Errors are detected within minutes rather than discovered days later
Recovery from any error category takes less than 15 minutes
Rollback procedures exist and have been tested for all state-modifying operations
Error frequency decreases over time as patterns are identified and prevented
failure_modes: No checkpoints: errors compound through long execution chains before detection
Recovery procedures that exist on paper but have never been tested
Panic response: overreacting to errors by rolling back more than necessary or adding excessive process
Root cause neglect: recovering from the symptom without investigating why the error occurred

sub_methods.name: Escalation Timeout
sub_methods.description: If an automated or AI-driven approach has not produced results within a defined time window, stop and reassess. The timeout prevents the sunk-cost trap where teams invest hours debugging an approach that should have been abandoned in minutes. Define timeouts per task type: 15 minutes for routine operations, 30 minutes for complex debugging, 60 minutes maximum before mandatory reassessment.

sub_methods.name: Manual Fallback
sub_methods.description: Sometimes the correct engineering answer is to do it by hand. When automation fails repeatedly, when the debugging time exceeds the manual execution time, when the edge case is genuinely unique, the manual fallback is not a failure of engineering. It is a recognition that not every problem justifies an automated solution. Document when and why you fell back to manual so the pattern is visible.

sub_methods.name: Root Cause vs Workaround
sub_methods.description: When recovering from an error, explicitly identify whether you are fixing the root cause or applying a workaround. Both are valid, but they have different implications. A root cause fix prevents recurrence. A workaround gets you moving but leaves the underlying issue intact. Document which one you chose and why. Workarounds that are not tracked become permanent technical debt.

techniques.name: Anti-Spin Protocol
techniques.description: When an agent is blocked, the correct response is to escalate collaboration, not escalate force. The complexity gradient has a speed limit, and that speed limit has a human checkpoint. One failed attempt at the obvious approach, then stop and check in. Pitch the complex solution before executing it. This prevents the pattern where agents burn hours on increasingly elaborate workarounds for problems that a thirty-second human conversation would resolve.
techniques.implementation: Define a clear escalation sequence: (1) try the obvious approach, (2) if it fails, describe the problem and proposed alternatives to the human, (3) the human selects or redirects, (4) execute the approved approach. Never attempt more than one complex workaround without human approval. Track incidents where force was escalated instead of collaboration, and review them to refine the protocol.
success_indicators: Blocked tasks are resolved through conversation rather than brute force
Time-to-resolution for blocked tasks decreases over time
Agents present options rather than executing increasingly desperate workarounds
The human is consulted at decision points, not informed of decisions already made
failure_modes: Agent pride: treating escalation as failure rather than protocol, leading to increasingly creative but unproductive attempts
Over-escalation: asking the human about trivially solvable problems, degrading trust and wasting human attention
Spin disguised as progress: the agent reports 'trying alternative approaches' while burning cycles on variations of the same failed strategy
Human unavailability: the escalation target is not responsive, and the agent has no fallback protocol

sub_methods.name: One-and-Check
sub_methods.description: One failed attempt at the obvious approach, then check in with the human operator. Do not attempt a second complex approach without approval. The first failure contains diagnostic information that the human needs to see. Presenting 'I tried X and it failed because Y, here are options Z1 and Z2' is always more productive than 'I tried X, Y, Z1, Z2, and Z3, they all failed, what now?'

sub_methods.name: Pitch Before Execute
sub_methods.description: Complex solutions must be proposed to the human before execution begins. Describe the approach, the expected time investment, the risk, and the alternatives. The human may know a simpler path. The human may know the approach will not work. The human may redirect entirely. Any of these outcomes saves more time than executing a complex solution that turns out to be wrong.

sub_methods.name: Spin Detection
sub_methods.description: Recognize the signs of spinning: multiple attempts at variations of the same approach, increasing complexity without increasing results, time investment growing while confidence shrinking. When you detect spin in yourself or your agent, invoke the protocol immediately. Spin is not persistence. It is the sunk cost fallacy applied to problem-solving.

techniques.name: Scope Lock
techniques.description: Mid-execution ideas get captured and deferred, never integrated into the active sprint. The human will have ideas during building. The agent will see opportunities for improvement. Both are correct to notice them and wrong to act on them. Scope Lock separates the creative impulse from the execution discipline: every idea gets written down, tagged for later, and kept out of the current work. The sprint stays clean.
techniques.implementation: Maintain a scratchpad or backlog file where mid-sprint ideas are captured with timestamps and context. When an idea surfaces during execution, write it to the backlog immediately, then return to the current task without discussion. Agents should flag scope creep the same way they flag test failures. Schedule dedicated creative sessions where backlog items are reviewed, refined, and scoped for future sprints.
success_indicators: Sprint scope does not grow after the plan is set
Ideas are captured reliably and reviewed in dedicated sessions
Agents flag scope expansion attempts without being asked
Backlog items that survive review become well-scoped tasks, not vague aspirations
failure_modes: Idea suppression: treating scope lock as 'no new ideas allowed' instead of 'new ideas go here, not there'
Backlog neglect: capturing ideas but never reviewing them, eroding trust in the system
Scope creep through the back door: small additions that individually seem harmless but collectively double the sprint
Rigid enforcement on trivial changes: applying scope lock to a five-minute fix that genuinely belongs in the current task

sub_methods.name: Backlog Capture
sub_methods.description: Every mid-sprint idea is written to a dedicated backlog file with a timestamp, the context that sparked it, and a deferral tag. The capture must take under sixty seconds. If it takes longer, you are evaluating the idea, not capturing it. Capture now, evaluate later.

sub_methods.name: Agent Enforcement
sub_methods.description: Agents actively monitor for scope expansion during execution. When the human proposes a new feature, enhancement, or improvement during a sprint, the agent responds with acknowledgment and deferral: captured to backlog, continuing with current task. The agent does not evaluate whether the idea is good enough to break scope. All ideas break scope equally.

sub_methods.name: Creative Review Sessions
sub_methods.description: Schedule dedicated sessions for reviewing the backlog. These are not sprints. They are design meetings where captured ideas are evaluated, refined, combined, or discarded. The separation between capturing and evaluating is the mechanism that makes Scope Lock work. Ideas that seemed urgent during a sprint often look different when reviewed with full context.

techniques.name: Testing Pyramid
techniques.description: Implement a multi-tiered testing strategy that validates AI-generated output at every level. The Testing Pyramid applies traditional software testing principles to human-AI collaboration: unit-level validation of individual outputs, integration testing of combined outputs, system testing of complete deliverables, and acceptance testing against human expectations. More tests at the base, fewer at the top, comprehensive coverage throughout.
techniques.implementation: Define 3-4 testing tiers appropriate to your domain. Typically: (1) Unit/Atomic: individual output validation, (2) Integration: combined output coherence, (3) System: complete deliverable functionality, (4) Acceptance: meets human expectations. Automate lower tiers. Maintain human review at the acceptance tier. Track test coverage and failure patterns.
success_indicators: Testing catches issues before they reach production
Lower-tier tests run automatically and frequently
Test failure patterns inform improvements to agent instructions
Test coverage is comprehensive without being burdensome
failure_modes: Only testing at the acceptance level: issues found too late
Inverted pyramid: manual testing heavy, automated testing light
Tests that pass but don't validate meaningful properties
Test maintenance overhead exceeding the value of testing

sub_methods.name: Atomic Validation Layer
sub_methods.description: The pyramid base: high-volume, fast, automated tests that validate individual outputs in isolation. Structural checks, type validation, constraint enforcement, and format verification. These tests run on every change and catch the majority of issues. Optimize for speed; if atomic tests are slow, they will not run frequently enough.

sub_methods.name: Integration Coherence Testing
sub_methods.description: The pyramid middle: tests that validate how individual outputs combine into larger deliverables. Cross-reference checks, consistency validation across related outputs, and interface contract verification. These catch issues that atomic tests miss: individually correct outputs that are collectively incoherent.

sub_methods.name: System Experience Validation
sub_methods.description: The pyramid upper tier: end-to-end tests that validate complete deliverables as a user would experience them. Navigate full workflows, verify user-facing behavior, and confirm that the assembled product works as intended. Fewer tests than lower tiers, but each validates a critical user path.

sub_methods.name: Human Acceptance Review
sub_methods.description: The pyramid apex: human judgment applied to deliverables that pass all automated tiers. Some qualities cannot be automated: aesthetic quality, narrative coherence, strategic alignment, and user experience require human evaluation. This tier focuses human attention on what only humans can judge, leaving verified items to automation.

techniques.name: Code Quality Standards
techniques.description: Establish explicit standards for the quality of AI-generated code. AI agents can write code quickly, but quality requires explicit standards: read existing code before modifying it, follow established patterns, don't introduce new dependencies without justification, and test before committing. Code Quality Standards convert implicit expectations into explicit, enforceable rules that agents follow consistently.
techniques.implementation: Document 10-15 code quality rules as standing orders. Common rules: explore before proposing changes, follow existing patterns, minimize new dependencies, write tests for new functionality, don't refactor beyond the task scope, preserve existing style. Enforce through code review and automated linting. Update rules based on observed quality issues.
success_indicators: AI-generated code follows project conventions consistently
Code review findings decrease over time as standards are internalized
New code integrates smoothly with existing codebase
Quality standards evolve based on observed issues, not assumptions
failure_modes: Standards too strict: impeding velocity without proportional quality gain
Standards not enforced: exist on paper but not in practice
Standards focused on style over substance: formatting over correctness
One-size-fits-all standards that don't account for different code contexts

techniques.name: Contributor Review Period
techniques.description: Require new contributors (human or AI) to operate under enhanced review for a defined period before granting full autonomy. The Review Period exists for calibration. New contributors need time to learn project conventions, and the team needs time to assess the contributor's quality, reliability, and alignment with project standards. The review period formalizes this mutual calibration.
techniques.implementation: Define a review period length (1-4 weeks recommended). During this period, all contributions undergo enhanced review. Define clear graduation criteria: consistency with project standards, zero critical issues, demonstrated understanding of governance. Communicate the process transparently: this is calibration, not probation.
success_indicators: New contributors produce higher-quality work after the review period
The team identifies and addresses contributor-specific issues early
Graduation criteria are clear and objective
Contributors view the period as supportive, not punitive
failure_modes: Review period too long: contributor fatigue before graduation
Review period too short: issues not identified before autonomy is granted
No graduation criteria: review period becomes indefinite
Review without feedback: finding issues but not helping the contributor improve

techniques.name: Cross-Platform Validation
techniques.description: Test AI-generated artifacts across all target platforms, environments, and configurations. AI agents typically work in one environment but their output may need to function across many. Cross-Platform Validation ensures that output is tested in every context it will encounter, well beyond the development environment. This includes operating systems, browsers, screen sizes, accessibility tools, and deployment environments.
techniques.implementation: Define the matrix of platforms and environments your output must support. Create test procedures for each platform. Automate cross-platform testing where possible. Test in the most constrained environment first: issues found there often affect all platforms. Track platform-specific issues to identify patterns.
success_indicators: Output functions correctly across all target platforms
Platform-specific issues are caught before deployment
Cross-platform testing is integrated into the regular workflow, not an afterthought
The platform support matrix is documented and current
failure_modes: Testing only on the development platform: works for me syndrome
Platform matrix too ambitious: testing more platforms than you actually support
Automated tests that don't reflect real platform behavior
Discovering platform issues only after deployment

techniques.name: Audit Trail System
techniques.description: Maintain comprehensive, searchable records of all significant actions, decisions, and changes. An Audit Trail supports both compliance and learning. When something goes wrong, the audit trail enables root cause analysis. When something goes right, it enables pattern replication. Every significant action should leave a mark in the record: who did it, what they did, why they did it, and when.
techniques.implementation: Define what constitutes a 'significant action' (commits, configuration changes, access grants, policy changes, incident responses). Establish a logging format that captures actor, action, context, and timestamp. Store logs in a searchable, persistent system. Review logs periodically to identify patterns. Use log data to improve processes.
success_indicators: Significant actions are logged consistently and automatically
Audit data is searchable and accessible to authorized personnel
Root cause analysis is possible for any incident
Audit data informs process improvements
failure_modes: Logging everything: signal buried in noise
Logging without reviewing: data accumulates but is never analyzed
Inconsistent logging: some actions captured, others missed
Audit data not accessible when needed: stored but not searchable

techniques.name: Output Verification
techniques.description: Systematic methods for detecting inaccurate, fabricated, or misleading content in AI-generated output. RLHF-tuned language models tend to be overconfident, their expressed confidence poorly calibrated against actual accuracy: they produce fluent, assured output regardless of whether it is correct. Hallucinated facts, invented citations, plausible-sounding but incorrect technical claims, and subtly wrong data are indistinguishable from correct output without explicit verification. Output verification is the minimum standard for using AI-generated content in any context where accuracy matters.
techniques.implementation: Implement verification at three levels: structural (output matches requested format and constraints), factual (claims are verifiable against authoritative sources), and logical (conclusions follow from premises). Use cross-agent verification, in which a second model independently checks the output against source material, an application of the LLM-as-a-judge pattern to groundedness rather than preference scoring. Establish a verification threshold (what percentage of claims must be verified before acceptance) and adjust it based on the consequence of errors.
success_indicators: Factual errors are caught before output reaches production or publication
Verification coverage is proportional to the consequence of inaccuracy
Cross-agent verification catches errors that self-review misses
The team maintains a running log of error types for pattern detection
failure_modes: Verification theater: reviewing output without actually checking claims against sources
Over-trust in confident language: agents that express certainty are no more likely to be correct
Single-point verification: one agent checking another without independent source access
Verification fatigue: skipping checks on routine output that later proves to contain errors

techniques.name: Structured History System
techniques.description: Maintain a structured, versioned record of project history including plans, decisions, and health metrics. The history system serves as the institutional memory of the collaboration. It records what happened and why: the decisions made, the alternatives considered, and the lessons learned. When context is lost (as it inevitably is in AI collaboration), the history system provides recovery.
techniques.implementation: Create a structured project history system with clear organization. Include mechanisms for quick scanning of current state, tracking of active and completed work, and archival of deferred plans. Ensure entries are structured for fast retrieval. Update at session end and use as a primary input during context recovery.
success_indicators: The history system accurately reflects current project state
Context recovery starts with history review
Decision rationale is preserved and accessible
The history system is updated consistently, not sporadically
failure_modes: History not updated: becomes stale and untrustworthy
History too detailed: quick scanning is impossible
History maintained by one person: single point of failure
History disconnected from actual work: a separate document nobody reads

sub_methods.name: YAML Frontmatter Protocol
sub_methods.description: Every history document gets machine-readable frontmatter with standardized fields: plan name, status, owner, key decision, and date. This enables fast scanning: agents can read frontmatter (under 50 tokens) to determine relevance before committing to reading the full document (500+ tokens). Frontmatter is the index layer of the history system.

sub_methods.name: Lifecycle State Management
sub_methods.description: Track every plan through explicit lifecycle states: active, complete, deferred, archived. State transitions are documented with timestamps and rationale. The lifecycle ensures nothing is silently abandoned: deferred plans have documented reasons, completed plans have summaries, and archived plans remain accessible.

sub_methods.name: Cross-Reference Validation
sub_methods.description: Maintain bidirectional references between the history system and the actual work (code commits, deployed artifacts, published content). If the history says a plan is complete, the corresponding artifacts should exist. If artifacts exist without history entries, the history has gaps. Regular cross-reference audits catch drift.

techniques.name: Session Handoff Protocol
techniques.description: Define a structured format for transferring context between sessions. Every session end is a potential knowledge cliff: if context isn't captured, the next session starts from scratch. The Session Handoff Protocol creates a standardized 'shift change brief' that captures: what was accomplished, what's in progress, what decisions are pending, and what context the next session needs to continue seamlessly.
techniques.implementation: Create a handoff template with 4-6 fields: Accomplished (what shipped), In Progress (what's partially done), Pending Decisions (what needs resolution), Context (what the next session needs to know), Blockers (what's preventing progress). Complete the handoff at every session end. Store handoffs where the next session will read them during context recovery.
success_indicators: Next-session startup time is minimized by quality handoffs
No work is lost between sessions due to context gaps
Handoffs capture enough context for a different agent to continue the work
Handoff quality is consistent across sessions
failure_modes: Handoffs skipped under time pressure: exactly when they're most needed
Handoffs too brief: next session can't reconstruct context
Handoffs too verbose: next session spends more time reading than working
Handoffs stored where the next session doesn't read them

techniques.name: Institutional Memory Repository
techniques.description: Create a persistent, organized repository of institutional knowledge that survives session resets, model changes, and team transitions. The Repository is the collaboration's long-term, external memory: accumulated knowledge that lives permanently, outside any single session's context window. It includes agent profiles, governance documents, operational procedures, and reference materials. Unlike session-specific context, the Repository's contents are always available and always current.
techniques.implementation: Create a dedicated repository structure for institutional knowledge. Organize by category: governance, procedures, profiles, reference materials. Version control all contents. Establish ownership and update responsibilities. Include the repository in context recovery protocols. Audit quarterly for accuracy and relevance.
success_indicators: Institutional knowledge survives session resets and model changes
New team members can onboard from the repository alone
Repository contents are accurate, current, and well-organized
The repository is referenced regularly in day-to-day work
failure_modes: Repository becomes a write-only medium: updated but not read
No ownership: nobody is responsible for accuracy and currency
Repository organization degrades over time without maintenance
Knowledge scattered across multiple locations instead of consolidated

techniques.name: Decision Records
techniques.description: Document significant decisions with their context, rationale, alternatives considered, and expected consequences. Decisions without documented rationale become mysterious over time: the team knows what was decided but not why, making it impossible to evaluate whether the decision is still appropriate. Decision Records preserve the thinking behind choices so they can be revisited intelligently.
techniques.implementation: Create a decision record template with: Context (the situation that required a decision), Decision (what was decided), Rationale (why this option was chosen), Alternatives (what else was considered and why it was rejected), Consequences (expected outcomes). Record decisions when they're made, not after the fact. Store in the project history system or institutional memory repository.
success_indicators: Past decisions can be understood and evaluated with full context
The team can distinguish between decisions that should be revisited and those that remain sound
Decision patterns are identifiable across the project history
New team members understand both the current state and how it came to be
failure_modes: Recording decisions after the fact: rationale is reconstructed, not captured
Recording only the decision without alternatives: can't evaluate the choice
Decision records not findable when needed
Treating all decisions equally: trivial decisions documented with same rigor as critical ones

techniques.name: Journey Preservation
techniques.description: Systematically capture and preserve war stories, breakthroughs, failures, and journey documentation. The collaboration's story: the messy, real, human story of building with AI: is itself valuable content. Journey Preservation ensures that significant moments are captured in real-time: the bug that led to a governance overhaul, the breakthrough that unlocked a new capability, the failure that taught the most important lesson. The journey is the content.
techniques.implementation: Create a trigger system for capturing significant moments: breakthroughs, failures, funny incidents, lessons learned. Capture in real-time, not after the fact: the details fade quickly. Store in a persistent collection with enough context to reconstruct the story later. Tag by theme for retrieval. Review periodically for content that can be shared externally.
success_indicators: Significant moments are captured when they happen
War stories contain enough detail to be useful and engaging
The collection grows organically as the collaboration produces stories
Captured moments inform future decision-making and external communication
failure_modes: Capturing everything: drowning in stories with no curation
Capturing nothing: relying on memory to reconstruct significant moments
Capturing too late: details lost between the event and the recording
Stories captured but never used: a collection nobody reads

sub_methods.name: Real-Time Capture Triggers
sub_methods.description: Define specific moments that should trigger immediate capture: breakthroughs, failures, governance incidents, notable moments, and key insights. Implement a low-friction capture mechanism (single command or keyword) that persists the moment with minimal interruption to workflow. Capture delay is the enemy; details fade within minutes.

sub_methods.name: Multi-Layer Persistence
sub_methods.description: Store captured moments in multiple locations for redundancy and different access patterns: a permanent archive (blog/vault), a cross-session memory layer (auto-memory), and a curated collection (war stories). Each layer serves a different retrieval need: quick reference, deep research, and external publication.

sub_methods.name: Content Potential Tagging
sub_methods.description: Tag every captured moment with its potential reuse contexts: blog post, podcast episode, book chapter, conference talk, team training material. This transforms the capture collection from a passive archive into an active content pipeline. Review tagged content periodically to identify patterns and themes.

techniques.name: Redundant Backup Strategy
techniques.description: Implement multiple, independent backup mechanisms for all critical data and knowledge. In human-AI collaboration, context loss is the ever-present threat. A single backup is not a backup: it's a single point of failure. Redundant Backup Strategy ensures that critical knowledge exists in at least three independent locations, using at least two different mechanisms, with regular verification that backups are current and restorable.
techniques.implementation: Identify all critical data (source code, knowledge repositories, governance documents, session histories). Establish three backup locations using at least two different mechanisms (e.g., git + cloud sync + local copy). Automate backup where possible. Verify backup integrity monthly. Document restoration procedures and test them quarterly.
success_indicators: Critical data exists in 3+ independent locations
Backups are automated and verified regularly
Restoration procedures are documented and tested
The team has recovered from at least one data loss event using backups
failure_modes: Backups exist but have never been tested for restoration
All backups in the same location: single point of failure
Backups not current: restoring stale data
Backup procedures documented but not automated: relies on human memory

techniques.name: Context Window Management
techniques.description: Strategic management of what information occupies the finite context window available to AI agents during a session. The context window is the single most important technical constraint in AI collaboration. It determines what the agent knows, what it has forgotten, and what it will hallucinate to fill gaps. Effective context window management prioritizes high-value information, compacts or evicts stale context, and structures loading sequences to maximize signal-to-noise ratio within the available token budget.
techniques.implementation: Audit the token cost of every piece of context loaded at session start. Rank context by utility: governance rules and active task state first, reference material and historical context second, supplementary material last. Implement progressive loading: start with essential context and add detail only when the task requires it. Monitor for context window pressure indicators: agents repeating themselves, forgetting earlier instructions, or producing output inconsistent with loaded context.
success_indicators: Agents operate within their effective context window without degradation
Context loading is prioritized by task relevance, not loading order habit
Stale or irrelevant context is identified and removed between task phases
The team can articulate the token cost of their standard context loading
failure_modes: Loading everything: filling the context window with comprehensive but low-relevance material, leaving no room for task execution
No prioritization: treating all context as equally important, so critical information is pushed out by volume
Ignoring degradation signals: continuing to add context after agents show signs of window pressure
Static loading: using the same context regardless of task type

sub_methods.name: Shallow-First Loading
sub_methods.description: Load YAML frontmatter and metadata before full document content. A shallow scan of 50 tokens per file can determine relevance before committing 5,000 tokens to the full document. This pattern applies to any context loading: read the index before reading the chapters, read the summary before reading the report, read the frontmatter before reading the post.

sub_methods.name: Traceable Paths
sub_methods.description: Every shallow reference must point to its deep context. When you load a summary, include the file path to the full document. When you load frontmatter, include the route to the complete content. Traceability means the agent can always go deeper when needed without having to search for where the detail lives.

sub_methods.name: Context Budget
sub_methods.description: Measure the token cost of your standard context loading and set a budget. If your context window is 200K tokens and your governance plus project state consumes 60K, you have 140K for task execution. Know this number. Track it. When loading decisions compete for budget, prioritize by task relevance. The budget is the constraint that forces prioritization.

techniques.name: Operational Flywheel
techniques.description: Work output feeds back into system improvement. Every task, challenge, or engagement produces artifacts that can be captured, structured, and used to make the system better at the next task. This is a data flywheel: solve problems, capture each solution as structured data, use that data to improve tooling or fine-tune the model, let the improved system take on harder problems, and repeat. It is a concrete pipeline for continual improvement in which today's work becomes tomorrow's training signal.
techniques.implementation: Define the capture format before starting work. Build capture into the workflow, not after it. Every solved problem produces structured output in a consistent format that feeds downstream systems. Measure the flywheel: track how much structured data each work session produces, and how downstream systems improve as the corpus grows. The pipeline must be automated enough that capture does not significantly slow execution.
success_indicators: Every work session produces structured artifacts that feed improvement pipelines
Downstream systems measurably improve as the corpus grows
Time-to-solve decreases on comparable difficulty tasks over time
The team naturally captures insights during work rather than reconstructing them after
failure_modes: Capture overhead that slows execution to the point where the flywheel costs more than it returns
Capturing volume without quality: large amounts of low-value data that does not improve downstream systems
Pipeline breaks: data is captured but never processed, reviewed, or integrated
Reconstructing instead of capturing: trying to document what happened after the session ends, producing degraded versions of insights that were sharp in the moment

sub_methods.name: Capture-During-Execution
sub_methods.description: Structured data is captured during the work, not reconstructed afterward. Notes degrade fast. The insight that was perfectly clear during execution is vague and incomplete two hours later. Build capture into the execution workflow so that the structured output is a byproduct of the work, not an additional task.

sub_methods.name: Format-First Pipeline
sub_methods.description: Define the output format before starting work. When the format is defined upfront, capture becomes mechanical rather than creative, reducing overhead and increasing consistency. The format is the contract between the capture step and every downstream consumer.

sub_methods.name: Flywheel Metrics
sub_methods.description: Measure the flywheel directly: structured artifacts per work session, downstream improvement per corpus increment, time-to-solve trends on comparable tasks. If the flywheel is not producing measurable improvement, either the capture quality is too low, the processing pipeline is broken, or the downstream system cannot learn from the data being provided. Diagnose and fix.

techniques.name: Gradient Execution
techniques.description: Structured priority ordering for complex problem-solving: identify the target, version it, check for known vulnerabilities, harvest credentials, escalate privileges, THEN deep dive into custom work. Each step narrows the search space before committing effort to the next. The gradient ensures you run cheap, high-signal checks before expensive exploration. Applies to any domain where enumeration precedes exploitation: security, debugging, data analysis, research.
techniques.implementation: Before starting execution, define the gradient for your domain. List checks from cheapest/fastest to most expensive/slowest. Execute in order, recording findings at each step. Only proceed to the next level when the current level is exhausted or points definitively to deeper work. Build the gradient into your runbooks so it becomes automatic, not a decision you make under pressure.
success_indicators: Known solutions are found before custom work begins
Time spent on expensive deep dives decreases because cheap checks catch most issues
Execution follows a repeatable priority order rather than ad-hoc exploration
Team members can articulate their gradient and why each step is ordered where it is
failure_modes: Skipping early gradient steps because the problem looks complex, missing the easy win
Gradient too rigid: refusing to jump ahead when strong evidence points to a specific cause
Not updating the gradient as new cheap checks become available
Treating the gradient as a checklist rather than a priority ordering, wasting time on irrelevant steps

sub_methods.name: Domain Gradient Mapping
sub_methods.description: Define the specific gradient for your domain. In security: identify, version, CVE check, creds, privesc, custom exploitation. In debugging: reproduce, logs, known issues, instrumentation, deep trace. Each domain has a natural gradient from cheap to expensive. Map it once, refine as you learn.

sub_methods.name: Step Gate Discipline
sub_methods.description: At each gradient step, explicitly decide: proceed deeper or stop here. Record what the current step found and why the next step is necessary. This prevents skipping ahead on instinct and creates an audit trail of the execution path.

sub_methods.name: Gradient Refinement
sub_methods.description: After each engagement, review the gradient. Did a step consistently produce nothing? Move it down or remove it. Did you keep finding issues that should have been caught earlier? Add a step. The gradient evolves with your domain knowledge.

techniques.name: Structured Model Evaluation
techniques.description: Head-to-head model comparison using consistent prompt sets, automated metrics, and structured JSON results. Multiple candidates are evaluated against the same benchmarks, scored on defined dimensions, and the winner advances. This is model selection through controlled experiment, not vibes. Every evaluation produces a reusable artifact: the prompt set, the scoring rubric, and the results archive.
techniques.implementation: Build a reusable evaluation runner. Define your prompt set covering the dimensions that matter for your use case. Score each candidate on consistent axes: accuracy, brevity, structure, methodology, factual correctness. Automate what you can (word count, format compliance, canary leak detection, token throughput). Record results as structured JSON. Run the same eval on every candidate. Winner advances, losers are documented with specific failure modes.
success_indicators: Model selection decisions are backed by reproducible evaluation data
Evaluation prompt sets grow and improve with each round
Results are structured and comparable across candidates and time periods
The team can articulate why the current model was chosen with specific metrics
failure_modes: Evaluating on too few prompts, producing statistically meaningless results
Scoring dimensions that do not reflect real-world use, optimizing for the wrong thing
Not automating: manual evaluation does not scale and introduces inconsistency
Single-round evaluation without re-testing as models update and capabilities shift

sub_methods.name: Evaluation Prompt Set Design
sub_methods.description: Build a prompt set that covers the dimensions you care about. Include easy baseline prompts, domain-specific challenges, edge cases, and adversarial inputs. The prompt set is a reusable asset. Version it. Grow it after each evaluation round based on what you learned.

sub_methods.name: Automated Scoring Pipeline
sub_methods.description: Automate every metric you can: word count, format compliance, token throughput, canary leak detection, response time. Manual scoring for subjective dimensions (quality, methodology) but automate the rest. The scoring pipeline should produce a structured JSON result per candidate per prompt.

sub_methods.name: Tournament Progression
sub_methods.description: Structure evaluations as tournament rounds. Winner of each round advances to face the next challenger. Document why each loser was eliminated with specific failure modes. The tournament archive becomes a decision log for model selection history.

techniques.name: Dual-Lane Session Architecture
techniques.description: Running parallel session lanes with distinct domain boundaries, where each lane has its own identity, scope, and update protocol. The key insight beyond basic multi-session is that sessions need domain boundaries as well as coordination protocols. Each lane owns a domain and cannot accidentally modify the other's state. Coordination happens through shared state files rather than direct session interaction.
techniques.implementation: Define your lanes by domain, not by task. Each lane gets a name, a scope boundary, and a list of what it owns. Establish a shared state file (STATUS.md or equivalent) that both lanes read and update. Neither lane touches files or systems owned by the other. When lanes need to communicate, they do it through the shared state, never through direct session interaction. Review lane boundaries when domain scopes shift.
success_indicators: Zero cross-lane conflicts over sustained operation
Each lane can describe its domain boundary without ambiguity
Shared state is the only coordination mechanism, and it works reliably
Both lanes ship independently without blocking each other
failure_modes: Lanes defined by task instead of domain, leading to boundary violations when tasks overlap
Shared state becomes a bottleneck or source of conflicts
Lanes diverge in context, losing awareness of what the other has shipped
Over-coordination: lanes spending more time syncing than working

sub_methods.name: Domain Boundary Definition
sub_methods.description: Explicitly define what each lane owns: which files, which systems, which concerns. The boundary is a contract. If it is ambiguous, lanes will collide. Write it down and enforce it.

sub_methods.name: Shared State Protocol
sub_methods.description: Define how the shared state file is read and updated. Each lane reads at session start, updates at session end. If the state has changed since you read it, incorporate those changes before overwriting. The protocol prevents lost updates without requiring real-time coordination.

sub_methods.name: Lane Identity Assignment
sub_methods.description: Give each lane a name and identity that makes the domain obvious. BUILD/SHIP, TRAIN/DEPLOY, EXPLORE/HARDEN. The name should immediately communicate what the lane does and does not own. Identity prevents scope drift.

techniques.name: Certification-Driven Knowledge Extraction
techniques.description: Using structured certification and training programs as knowledge extraction pipelines. As the human works through labs and modules, AI extracts training pairs, methodology notes, and technique documentation in real-time. The certification provides ground truth (labs validate answers), the AI provides extraction speed, and the corpus grows with every module completed. Each lab answer becomes a validated technique description. Each module becomes a structured knowledge domain.
techniques.implementation: Before starting a certification or training program, set up the extraction pipeline: define the output format for training pairs, assign the AI to extract as you work, and establish a quality check for each extracted pair. Work through the labs normally. After each lab, review the extracted pairs for accuracy. The certification validates the technical content; your review validates the extraction quality. Batch and integrate into your corpus at module boundaries.
success_indicators: Every certification module produces structured training pairs as a byproduct
Extraction happens during the work, not as a separate reconstruction effort
Corpus grows proportionally to certification progress with consistent quality
Extracted pairs pass validation against lab ground truth
failure_modes: Extraction overhead slowing lab completion to the point where the certification suffers
Extracting pairs without validating against lab results, producing plausible but incorrect data
Treating all labs as equal: some produce high-value pairs, others produce trivial ones
Not reviewing extracted pairs, allowing quality drift as the pipeline scales

sub_methods.name: Lab-Validated Pair Extraction
sub_methods.description: Each lab produces a question-answer pair where the lab itself validates the answer. The AI extracts the pair in your corpus format. The lab is the ground truth oracle. This is the cheapest, highest-confidence training data you can produce.

sub_methods.name: Module-Level Domain Structuring
sub_methods.description: Group extracted pairs by certification module to create domain-structured corpus segments. Each module maps to a knowledge domain. The certification's own structure becomes your corpus taxonomy.

sub_methods.name: Cross-Program Corpus Integration
sub_methods.description: When working through multiple certifications or training programs, integrate pairs across programs. Overlapping domains produce redundant pairs that validate quality. Gaps between programs highlight knowledge domains that need other sources.

techniques.name: Comparison-Driven Improvement
techniques.description: A deliberate loop: solve a challenge, write it up, compare your writeup against reference material, extract delta training pairs from the comparison, feed those pairs into model training, and solve the next challenge better. The deltas are a form of active learning: the disagreements between your approach and the reference are the most informative examples, and they form candidate preference pairs for methods like DPO. Each revolution produces both data and capability. The comparison step is where the real value lives: your approach versus the reference reveals methodology gaps, alternative techniques, and efficiency differences that pure self-review misses.
techniques.implementation: After completing a challenge, write your approach before reading any reference material. Then read the reference. Compare systematically: what did you do differently? What did the reference catch that you missed? What did you catch that the reference missed? Extract each delta as a structured training pair. Feed the pairs into your training pipeline. Track improvement across challenges to confirm the flywheel is turning.
success_indicators: Comparison step consistently produces additional training pairs beyond the initial writeup
Methodology gaps decrease across successive challenges in the same domain
The team has a growing library of delta pairs that represent real capability gaps
Time-to-solve improves on comparable-difficulty challenges over time
failure_modes: Reading the reference before writing your approach, contaminating the comparison
Surface-level comparison that misses methodology differences in favor of outcome differences
Not tracking improvement, so the flywheel turns but nobody knows if it is working
Comparison fatigue: skipping the delta extraction step when time is short, breaking the loop

sub_methods.name: Blind Writeup First
sub_methods.description: Always write your approach before reading the reference. This is non-negotiable. If you read the reference first, the comparison produces agreement, not deltas. The blind writeup captures your actual methodology, not a rationalization of the reference approach.

sub_methods.name: Systematic Delta Extraction
sub_methods.description: Compare along defined axes: tools used, methodology order, techniques applied, efficiency, and findings. For each axis, note what differed and why. Each meaningful delta becomes a training pair. Structure the extraction so it produces consistent, machine-readable output.

sub_methods.name: Revolution Tracking
sub_methods.description: Track metrics across flywheel revolutions: pairs produced per challenge, time-to-solve trend, delta frequency by category. If deltas in a category stop appearing, you have closed that gap. If they persist, the training is not transferring. Both signals are valuable.

techniques.name: Approval Gate Architecture
techniques.description: A structured human-in-the-loop (HITL) approval mechanism for autonomous agent actions. Before an agent executes any action, it displays what it intends to do, provides a risk assessment, and waits for human approval. The architecture supports trust graduation: start with full approval for every action, then graduate to auto-approve for low-risk actions as the agent proves reliability. The gate is human-in-the-loop oversight scaled by accumulated trust.
techniques.implementation: Build approval into the agent execution loop. Each proposed action gets: a clear description, risk level, expected outcome, and Y/N/skip controls. For destructive or irreversible actions, require explicit approval regardless of trust level. Track approval history. After N successful approvals in a category, offer auto-approve for that category. Build safety checks into the gate itself: block patterns like rm -rf, scope violations, or unauthorized network access before they reach the human.
success_indicators: Every agent action passes through the gate before execution
Trust graduation is measurable: auto-approve categories grow as reliability is demonstrated
Zero unapproved destructive actions reach execution
The approval UX is fast enough that it does not become a bottleneck for trusted action categories
failure_modes: Approval fatigue: too many low-risk approvals cause the human to rubber-stamp without reading
Gate too permissive: auto-approving categories before sufficient evidence of reliability
Missing the gate entirely: agent pathways that bypass approval due to implementation gaps
Risk assessment inaccuracy: labeling high-risk actions as low-risk, defeating the purpose

sub_methods.name: Action Display Protocol
sub_methods.description: Define how proposed actions are displayed to the human. Include syntax-highlighted code for script execution, network targets for scan actions, and file paths for write operations. The display must make the action's impact immediately clear without requiring the human to parse raw commands.

sub_methods.name: Trust Graduation Ladder
sub_methods.description: Define the thresholds for graduating from full approval to auto-approve. Track approvals by action category. After a defined number of consecutive successful approvals (no rejections, no incidents), offer auto-approve for that category. Any rejection resets the counter. The ladder makes trust accumulation visible and reversible.

sub_methods.name: Safety Check Layer
sub_methods.description: Build pattern-matching safety checks into the gate itself. Block known-dangerous patterns (recursive delete, scope violations, credential exposure) before they reach the human for approval. The safety layer catches the obvious threats so human attention can focus on the subtle ones.

techniques.name: Multi-Paradigm Learning Strategy
techniques.description: Apply multiple learning paradigms to the same experience to maximize knowledge extraction. For each engagement: SFT extracts technique descriptions for supervised fine-tuning, RL captures decision sequences as reward-scored tuples for reinforcement learning, and unsupervised methods cluster findings for anomaly detection and pattern discovery. Three paradigms, one engagement, three data products. The same experience yields fundamentally different training signals depending on which lens you apply.
techniques.implementation: Before each engagement, set up extraction for all three paradigms. SFT: define the pair format and extract technique descriptions from writeups. RL: instrument the agent loop to capture state-action-reward-state tuples with a defined reward function. Unsupervised: collect raw findings for post-engagement clustering and embedding analysis. Run all three extractions concurrently. After the engagement, process each paradigm's output through its own pipeline. Track which paradigm produces the most downstream improvement per engagement.
success_indicators: Each engagement produces structured output for at least two of three paradigms
SFT pairs, RL tuples, and clustering outputs all feed distinct downstream pipelines
The team can articulate which paradigm is producing the most value for their current needs
Paradigm-specific metrics improve over time as each pipeline matures
failure_modes: Extraction overhead for three paradigms exceeding the engagement's value, making the cure worse than the disease
One paradigm dominating while others are neglected, collapsing back to single-paradigm extraction
RL reward function misaligned with actual goals, producing tuples that train the wrong behavior
Unsupervised paradigm producing clusters without actionable insight, consuming resources for noise

sub_methods.name: SFT Pair Extraction
sub_methods.description: Extract question-answer training pairs from engagement writeups and lab results. Each technique described becomes a supervised fine-tuning example. Format consistency matters: define the pair schema once, use it everywhere, validate before ingestion.

sub_methods.name: RL Tuple Instrumentation
sub_methods.description: Instrument the agent execution loop to capture state-action-reward-next_state tuples. Define the reward function before the engagement: positive rewards for progress (service discovery, finding, access gained), negative rewards for waste (redundant scans, detection, scope violations). The tuples train decision-making in addition to knowledge.

sub_methods.name: Unsupervised Pattern Mining
sub_methods.description: After an engagement, cluster raw findings using embeddings, PCA, or other dimensionality reduction. Look for anomalies that do not fit known patterns, clusters that suggest new technique categories, and outliers that warrant deeper investigation. The unsupervised paradigm finds what you did not know to look for.

techniques.name: Eval-Gated Promotion
techniques.description: No new version reaches production on intuition. Eval-Gated Promotion requires every candidate model, agent, prompt, or tool to pass a defined evaluation suite before it replaces the incumbent. The suite is a fixed set of prompts or tasks, scored on the dimensions that matter, run identically against the current production version and each challenger. The version that wins on the metrics advances; the rest are documented with their specific failure modes. This turns version selection into a measured comparison rather than a judgment call.
techniques.implementation: Build a reusable evaluation suite covering the capabilities you depend on, with a scoring rubric for each. Define the promotion bar in advance: which metrics must improve, which must not regress, and by how much. Run the incumbent and every candidate through the same suite under the same conditions. Record results as structured data so comparisons are reproducible. Promote only the candidate that clears the bar, and archive the losers with their scores and failure modes as a decision record.
success_indicators: No version reaches production without a recorded eval result against the incumbent
Promotion decisions trace to specific metric improvements, not impressions
The eval suite is reused across versions, keeping comparisons consistent over time
Regressions are caught at the gate rather than discovered in production
failure_modes: Evaluating on a suite that does not reflect real production use, so the gate passes versions that fail in the field
Moving the promotion bar to fit a favored candidate instead of holding it fixed
Scoring only the dimensions that improved while ignoring the ones that regressed
Running the eval once and trusting it indefinitely as the workload and expectations drift

sub_methods.name: Fixed Promotion Bar
sub_methods.description: Define the pass criteria before running the evaluation: the metrics that must improve, the metrics that must not regress, and the minimum margin that counts as a real difference. Fixing the bar in advance prevents the bias of rationalizing whichever candidate you already preferred.

sub_methods.name: Incumbent Versus Challenger
sub_methods.description: Always score the current production version on the same suite, in the same run, as every challenger. A candidate's absolute score is meaningless without the incumbent as the baseline. The comparison is the decision; the absolute number is not.

sub_methods.name: Promotion Decision Record
sub_methods.description: Every promotion produces a record: the suite version, the scores for each candidate, the bar that was applied, and why the winner won. Losers are documented with their specific failure modes. The record makes the selection auditable and informs the next round.

techniques.name: Regression Guardrails
techniques.description: A new version can improve on one axis while silently degrading another. Regression Guardrails are a frozen, held-out set of checks that a change must continue to pass before it ships. Where Eval-Gated Promotion asks whether a candidate is better, the guardrail asks a separate question: did anything that used to work just break? The guardrail set is preserved unchanged across versions precisely so that it can detect when a capability you already had has regressed.
techniques.implementation: Curate a held-out set of cases that exercise capabilities you must not lose, including past failures you already fixed. Freeze it. Run it against every candidate before promotion. Treat any regression against the frozen set as a blocking failure, separate from the promotion metrics. When you genuinely retire a capability, retire its guardrail cases deliberately and record the decision; never silently drop a case to make a version pass.
success_indicators: Previously fixed failures do not reappear in later versions
The guardrail set is frozen and version-controlled, not edited to fit the candidate under test
A regression blocks promotion even when the candidate improves on other metrics
Retired guardrail cases are removed by explicit decision, with a recorded rationale
failure_modes: Editing or trimming the guardrail set so a desired version passes, which defeats its purpose
Folding the guardrail into the promotion metrics, so a strong average hides a specific regression
Never adding new cases, so capabilities gained after the set was frozen are unprotected
Treating a regression as acceptable collateral without an explicit, recorded decision to accept it

sub_methods.name: Frozen Held-Out Set
sub_methods.description: Maintain the guardrail cases as a frozen, version-controlled set, separate from the evaluation suite used for promotion. Freezing is the point: the set only detects regressions if it does not change to accommodate the version under test.

sub_methods.name: Fixed-Then-Protected Cases
sub_methods.description: Every defect you fix becomes a permanent guardrail case. The cheapest regression to catch is the one you already paid to fix once. Add the case the moment the fix lands, so the same failure cannot return unnoticed.

sub_methods.name: Blocking Semantics
sub_methods.description: A guardrail failure blocks promotion outright, independent of how the candidate scored on the improvement metrics. Keeping the two separate prevents a strong overall score from masking a specific, unacceptable regression.

techniques.name: Feedback Capture Loop
techniques.description: Improvement needs a signal, and the signal is feedback. The Feedback Capture Loop systematically records the human and automated judgments produced during normal work: corrections, approvals and rejections, preference between two outputs, and pass or fail against a verifier. Captured in a consistent format, these judgments become the training signal that drives the flywheels. These are the same kinds of signal that underlie supervised, preference-based, and reinforcement-style model training, gathered through daily practice rather than in a one-time labeling project.
techniques.implementation: Identify the points in your workflow where a judgment is already being made: a human approving or rejecting output, a reviewer choosing between alternatives, a test passing or failing. Capture each as structured feedback at the moment it happens, with enough context to be reusable. Distinguish the kinds of signal, because corrections, preferences, and verdicts feed different downstream uses. Route captured feedback into the curation and training pipelines rather than letting it evaporate in chat logs and review comments.
success_indicators: Routine approvals, rejections, and preferences are captured as structured signal, not lost in conversation
Each feedback record carries enough context to be reused without reconstruction
Feedback is typed by kind, so it feeds the right pipeline
Captured feedback reaches the curation pipeline at a measurable rate, not a sampled fraction
failure_modes: Feedback lives only in chat history and review threads, where it cannot be aggregated or reused
Capturing verdicts without context, producing signal that cannot be acted on later
Treating all feedback as one undifferentiated stream, so preference and correction signals are conflated
Collecting feedback that never reaches a pipeline, leaving the loop open rather than closed

sub_methods.name: Judgment-Point Instrumentation
sub_methods.description: Map the moments in the workflow where a human or an automated check already renders a judgment, and capture each one. The cheapest feedback is the judgment that is already being made; you only have to record it rather than manufacture it.

sub_methods.name: Typed Signal
sub_methods.description: Distinguish corrections (this is the right answer), preferences (this output is better than that one), and verdicts (this passed or failed). Each type feeds a different downstream use, and conflating them produces a stream that no pipeline can cleanly consume.

sub_methods.name: Closed-Loop Routing
sub_methods.description: Route captured feedback into the curation and training pipelines, and confirm it arrives. Feedback that is collected but never consumed is an open loop: it carries the cost of capture and none of the benefit.

techniques.name: Corpus Curation
techniques.description: The improvement corpus is the accumulated body of structured data the system learns from. Left to grow on its own, it collects duplicates, contradictions, and low-value entries that degrade the signal faster than volume improves it. Corpus Curation treats the corpus as a maintained asset: deduplicated, quality-gated at intake, versioned, and pruned. The quality of what the system learns is bounded by the quality of the corpus, so curation is the difference between a flywheel that compounds and one that drifts.
techniques.implementation: Define a quality gate that every entry must pass before it enters the corpus: format validity, source attribution, and a minimum quality bar. Deduplicate on intake and on a schedule. Version the corpus so any model or capability can be traced to the exact data it was built from. Prune entries that are stale, superseded, or shown to degrade downstream results. Measure corpus quality alongside size, and treat a growing corpus with falling quality as a regression.
success_indicators: Every corpus entry passes a defined quality gate before intake
Duplicates and contradictions are detected and resolved rather than accumulated
The corpus is versioned, so any trained capability is traceable to the exact data behind it
Corpus quality is measured, and growth never comes at the cost of declining signal
failure_modes: Accumulating volume without curation, so noise grows faster than signal
No intake gate, so malformed or low-value entries dilute the corpus
An unversioned corpus, making it impossible to trace a capability to its data or roll back a bad batch
Never pruning, so stale and superseded entries keep shaping training long after they should

sub_methods.name: Intake Quality Gate
sub_methods.description: Every candidate entry passes a defined gate before it joins the corpus: valid format, attributed source, and a minimum quality bar. The gate is cheaper than the cleanup, and an ungated corpus degrades the moment volume outpaces attention.

sub_methods.name: Deduplication And Conflict Resolution
sub_methods.description: Detect duplicate and contradictory entries on intake and on a schedule. Duplicates skew the signal toward whatever is repeated; contradictions teach the system both sides of a settled question. Resolve them rather than letting them accumulate.

sub_methods.name: Corpus Versioning
sub_methods.description: Version the corpus so that any model or capability can be traced to the exact data it was built from. Versioning makes results reproducible and makes it possible to roll back a bad batch instead of contaminating every version that follows.

techniques.name: Capability Versioning
techniques.description: Models, agents, and prompts change over time, and an undocumented change is an untraceable one. Capability Versioning applies the discipline of software versioning to the components that produce behavior: every model, agent profile, and significant prompt is versioned, reproducible from its inputs, and reversible to a known-good prior state. When behavior changes, versioning is what lets you say exactly what changed, compare it against what came before, and return to the previous version if the new one regresses.
techniques.implementation: Assign explicit versions to models, agent profiles, and significant prompts. Record what each version was built from: base, data, configuration, and parameters. Keep prior versions retrievable so any one can be restored. Tie behavior changes to version changes, so a shift in output can always be traced to a specific, documented change. Pair versioning with a tested rollback path, so reverting to the last known-good version is a routine operation rather than an emergency.
success_indicators: Every model, agent, and significant prompt carries an explicit version
Any version can be reproduced from its recorded inputs
A behavior change can be traced to the specific version change that caused it
Reverting to the previous known-good version is a tested, routine operation
failure_modes: Changing prompts or configurations in place with no version record, making behavior shifts untraceable
Versioning the model but not the prompt or agent profile, so a major behavior driver stays uncontrolled
Retaining no prior versions, so a regression cannot be rolled back
Recording version numbers without the inputs behind them, so versions are labeled but not reproducible

sub_methods.name: Reproducible Builds
sub_methods.description: Record what each version was built from: base, data, configuration, and parameters. A version number without its inputs is a label, not a build. Reproducibility is what makes a version something you can investigate and trust.

sub_methods.name: Version Everything That Drives Behavior
sub_methods.description: Version the model together with the agent profile and the significant prompts, because each is a driver of behavior. Controlling the model while changing the prompt in place leaves the largest behavior lever uncontrolled.

sub_methods.name: Rollback Readiness
sub_methods.description: Keep prior versions retrievable and the rollback path tested, so returning to the last known-good version is routine. This is the Reversibility Guarantee applied to capability: the handle has to work from the other side before you need it.

techniques.name: Methodology Evolution
techniques.description: The framework that governs the work must itself improve, or it ossifies into bureaucracy. Methodology Evolution treats the methodology as a versioned, evidence-driven artifact: methods are added when a pattern proves out across repeated practice, refined as evidence accumulates, and deprecated when superseded. Most methods exist because something went wrong and the system was built to prevent it, which means the methodology is never finished. It evolves on evidence drawn from the daily work it governs.
techniques.implementation: Treat the methodology as a versioned artifact with an explicit changelog. Add a method only when a pattern has proven itself across repeated practice, not on a single instance or a theoretical concern. Refine existing methods as evidence accumulates about where they succeed and fail. Deprecate methods that are superseded, and record why. Review the methodology on a regular cadence against the failures and successes of the period, so the framework that governs the work is shaped by the work itself.
success_indicators: New methods enter only after a pattern has proven out across repeated practice
The methodology has an explicit version and changelog, not silent edits
Methods are refined and deprecated on evidence, with recorded rationale
Each method can be traced to the real failure or success that motivated it
failure_modes: Adding methods from theory or a single incident, inflating the framework with untested process
Letting the methodology calcify, so it governs the work long after the work has moved on
Changing the methodology silently, so practitioners cannot tell what changed or why
Never deprecating, so superseded methods accumulate and dilute the ones that matter

sub_methods.name: Evidence-Gated Additions
sub_methods.description: A method earns its place only after the pattern it describes has proven out across repeated practice. Adding methods from a single incident or a theoretical worry inflates the framework with process that has not been tested against reality.

sub_methods.name: Versioned Changelog
sub_methods.description: Treat the methodology like any other versioned artifact: an explicit version, a changelog, and recorded rationale for every addition, refinement, and deprecation. Silent edits leave practitioners unable to tell what changed or trust that a change was deliberate.

sub_methods.name: Deprecation On Supersession
sub_methods.description: When a method is superseded, deprecate it deliberately and record why, rather than leaving it in place. Superseded methods that linger dilute the framework and send practitioners down paths the evidence has already abandoned.
